title: Wireless Security Protocols (WPA2, WPA3)
obj: Students will be able to compare wireless security protocols, configure WPA2/WPA3, and explain why older protocols are vulnerable.
content: WEP weaknesses
static keys, weak RC4 implementation, cracked in minutes; never use
WPA and WPA2
TKIP (WPA) → AES-CCMP (WPA2); Personal (PSK) vs. Enterprise (RADIUS); WPA2 still widely deployed
WPA3
SAE replaces PSK; forward secrecy; 192-bit enterprise mode; WPA3 transition mode for mixed clients
Enterprise authentication
802.1X, RADIUS server, EAP variants (PEAP, EAP-TLS); certificates vs. credentials
Common wireless attacks
evil twin, deauth attack, PMKID attack on WPA2; why WPA3 mitigates these
Best practices
disable WPS, use WPA3 where possible, strong PSK, separate IoT VLAN, monitor for rogue APs
assess: Protocol timeline: arrange WEP/WPA/WPA2/WPA3 chronologically and describe the key improvement each added
Lab: configure a router with WPA3-SAE and connect a client; verify the security mode in client adapter settings
Short answer: why is WPA2-Personal weaker than WPA2-Enterprise?

title: Malware Types, Detection, and Removal
obj: Students will be able to identify common malware types by behavior, use anti-malware tools to detect and remove infections, and restore a system to a clean state.
content: Malware taxonomy
virus (needs host), worm (self-propagating), trojan (disguised), ransomware, spyware, adware, rootkit, keylogger
Infection vectors
phishing email attachments, drive-by downloads, infected USB, malicious software bundles
Symptoms of infection
slow performance, unexpected pop-ups, disabled AV, unusual network traffic, encrypted files
Detection and scanning
real-time vs. on-demand scans; signature vs. heuristic/behavior detection; Windows Defender + Malwarebytes
Removal process
boot to Safe Mode, run offline scanner, quarantine vs. delete, check startup items and scheduled tasks
Post-removal steps
verify clean scan, patch the entry vector, change credentials, consider reimaging for rootkits
assess: Malware classification: given 8 behavior descriptions, identify the malware type
Removal lab: scan a deliberately infected (EICAR test file) VM and walk through the removal steps
Short answer: why might reimaging be preferable to cleaning a rootkit infection?

title: Social Engineering Attacks and Threats
obj: Students will be able to identify common social engineering attack types, explain why they succeed, and apply countermeasures to protect against them.
content: Phishing variants
phishing (bulk email), spear phishing (targeted), vishing (voice), smishing (SMS), whaling (executives)
Pretexting
fabricating a scenario to gain trust; impersonating IT, vendors, or management
Baiting and quid pro quo
dropping infected USB drives; offering fake tech support in exchange for access
Tailgating and piggybacking
following an authorized person through a secured door; why physical security training matters
Why social engineering works
authority, urgency, scarcity, social proof, familiarity — psychological triggers
Defenses
verification procedures, security awareness training, callback confirmation, reporting culture
assess: Attack identification: read 6 scenario descriptions and name the social engineering technique used
Phishing analysis: examine a sample phishing email and list 5 red flags
Short answer: why is security awareness training the primary defense against social engineering?

title: Workstation Security and Hardening
obj: Students will be able to apply a workstation hardening checklist including removing unnecessary software, configuring security policies, and verifying baseline security.
content: Hardening principles
reduce attack surface; disable unnecessary services, features, and accounts; apply least privilege
Remove unnecessary software
uninstall unused apps, disable optional Windows features, remove bloatware
Configure security policies
screen lock timeout, account lockout, audit logging, restrict USB storage
Application control
Software Restriction Policies or AppLocker; whitelisting approved applications
Host-based firewall
verify enabled, review inbound rules, block unused ports
Baseline and verification
security baseline tools (LGPO, CIS benchmarks); scanning with baseline compliance tool
assess: Hardening lab: apply a provided CIS-inspired checklist to a Windows VM and document each change
Before/after scan: run a vulnerability scan before and after hardening; count the reduction in findings
Short answer: what is the purpose of application whitelisting and what is its main operational drawback?

title: Mobile Device Security
obj: Students will be able to identify mobile security risks, configure basic protections, and explain how MDM enforces policy on mobile devices.
content: Mobile threat landscape
lost/stolen devices, malicious apps, unsecured Wi-Fi, SMS phishing, outdated OS
Screen lock and encryption
PIN/biometric requirements; iOS and Android full-disk encryption by default; when to enable
MDM and MAM
Mobile Device Management enforces policies (lock screen, encryption, remote wipe); MAM manages only apps
BYOD considerations
personal vs. corporate data separation, containerization (Android Work Profile, iOS Managed Apps)
App security
only install from official stores; review app permissions; sideloading risks
Remote wipe and locator
Find My (Apple), Find My Device (Google); selective wipe vs. full wipe; when to use each
assess: MDM policy design: given a company BYOD scenario, list 6 policy settings you would enforce via MDM
App permission audit: on a device or simulator, review the permissions of 3 installed apps and flag any excessive requests
Short answer: what is the difference between a selective wipe and a full wipe in MDM?

title: Data Destruction and Disposal Methods
obj: Students will be able to select the appropriate data destruction method for a given storage media type and regulatory context, and explain why deletion is not destruction.
content: Why deletion is not enough
deleted files remain on disk until overwritten; file system only removes directory entry
Overwriting (wiping)
DoD 5220.22-M, Gutmann method; single-pass with random data often sufficient for HDDs; tools (DBAN, Eraser)
SSD and flash challenges
wear leveling means data may persist in unreachable cells; manufacturer secure erase (ATA SE) preferred
Physical destruction
degaussing (HDD only), shredding, drilling, incineration; when to use each; chain of custody
Regulatory requirements
HIPAA, GDPR, PCI-DSS data disposal requirements; certificates of destruction
Asset disposal process
decommission checklist: wipe, document, certificate, physical disposal or donation
assess: Method selection: given 5 media types and disposal scenarios, select the most appropriate destruction method
Short answer: why is degaussing ineffective for SSDs?
Policy draft: write a 5-step data disposal SOP for a school that replaces 50 laptops per year

title: SOHO Network Security Settings
obj: Students will be able to configure a SOHO router with security best practices including changing defaults, enabling firewall, segmenting the network, and disabling insecure features.
content: Change default credentials
admin username and password on router; firmware update; disabling remote management
SSID and encryption
unique SSID, WPA3 or WPA2-AES, disable WEP/TKIP, disable WPS
Network segmentation
guest network isolation; IoT VLAN; keeping trusted devices separate from visitors and smart devices
Firewall and port forwarding
SPI firewall; only forward ports that are needed; UPnP risks; DMZ pitfalls
DHCP and DNS settings
custom DNS (Pi-hole, 1.1.1.1); DHCP reservations for key devices; disabling unused services
Monitoring and logging
router log review; connected device list; detecting unknown devices
assess: Router hardening lab: configure a consumer router (physical or simulated) against a provided security checklist
Connected device audit: list all devices on a network and identify any that shouldn't be there
Short answer: why should you disable UPnP on a home/small-office router?

title: Browser Security Settings
obj: Students will be able to configure browser security settings to protect against common web-based threats and assist end users with browser security issues.
content: HTTPS and certificate warnings
verifying padlock, certificate details, what to do when a cert is invalid, HSTS
Malicious site blocking
Safe Browsing (Chrome/Firefox), SmartScreen (Edge); how they work; reporting false positives
Pop-up and redirect blocking
legitimate vs. malicious pop-ups; override for trusted sites; drive-by download prevention
Saved passwords and autofill
risks of browser-saved passwords; recommending a password manager instead; clearing saved data
Extensions security
identifying malicious extensions; auditing permissions; enterprise-managed extension allowlist
Privacy settings
third-party cookie blocking, fingerprinting protection, private mode limitations; Firefox Enhanced Tracking Protection
assess: Browser audit: given a browser with insecure settings, apply a hardening checklist and document each change
Extension review: examine 3 extensions, note their permissions, and classify each as safe/concerning
Short answer: what information can a website collect even in private/incognito mode?
